title: Ajahn Funn: That we can live like this is due to the puñña (merit), vāsanā (latent tendencies), and pāramī (perfections) we have built up over…
content:
Dāna-Pāramī: Giving Up Much, Letting Go Much, Gains Great Reward

That we can live like this is due to the puñña (merit), vāsanā (latent tendencies), and pāramī (perfections) we have built up over many lives and many births.

And puñña and kusala (wholesome deeds) are nothing far away – they are precisely dāna-pāramī (perfection of generosity), sīla-pāramī (perfection of morality), and bhāvanā-pāramī (perfection of meditation).

We already know dāna: giving up, abandoning, letting go. Whoever gives up much, lets go much, gains great ānisaṃsa (reward). Whoever gives up little, lets go little, has little ānisaṃsa. Macchariya – stinginess, possessiveness – that is greed. We must give it up and become a generous donor. That is, we give away those very possessions, wealth, and objects – nothing else. It means to nurture oneself, just as the Perfectly Self-Enlightened Buddha, before attaining magga and phala (path and fruition), built up his perfections, including this dāna-pāramī. Understand this: dāna is our provision. When we have done enough of it, we need not fear traveling a long distance. It is like coming to this monastery here. If we haven't prepared anything, we have nothing. But if we have prepared, we need not fear hunger or hardship, fear suffering or difficulty.

title: Ajahn Funn: Ehipassiko means: "Come and see the Dhamma." So where shall we go to see it? To make this point understandable: when we speak…
content:
Ehipassiko: Come and See the Dhamma Within Yourself

Ehipassiko means: "Come and see the Dhamma." So where shall we go to see it?

To make this point understandable: when we speak of drawing inwards to see the Dhamma, it is precisely this Dhamma that we listen to. We listen because we all desire freedom from suffering. Suffering is right here — it is at ourselves, not anywhere far away. Therefore, we should all draw it inwards right here, to our own minds. Come and see this time — not see anything else.

Understand this well. When we say we are making merit, what is merit? You should all know this now. Do not just complain that you have no opportunity, no time. Right now you have the fullest opportunity. Your duties and all your work are gone. You have all come and are sitting here right now. Do not think about the past or the future. Listen and then contemplate right now.

If we have merit, it is right now. If we have kamma, wholesome or unwholesome, it is right now. We do not need to think about past or future. Where we will go in the future, we cannot know. The past has already passed; we have already left it behind. Now we must look at the present. This is what the Buddha taught: as the present is, so the future will be.

title: Ajahn Funn: Dhammo means the Buddha's teachings — the 84,000 Dhamma aggregates. We think they belong to the Buddha. We do not draw them inwards…
content:
Kusalā Dhammā and Akusalā Dhammā – We Are the Doers

Dhammo means the Buddha's teachings — the 84,000 Dhamma aggregates. We think they belong to the Buddha. We do not draw them inwards to ourselves, so we do not see them. Whatever we have not done for ourselves, we cannot rely on. We can rely only on what we have done. If we have done good, we rely on good. If we have done evil, we rely on evil. That is why the Pali says: kusalā dhammā, akusalā dhammā. We are the ones who have done all of them, aren't we? We have made merit, so we receive it.

Kusalā dhammā — kusala means skillfulness. All of you are skillful ones. Coming here, you have come to know what is kusala and akusala — that is, skillfulness. In summary, kusalā dhammā means our mind is good, our mind is happy, our mind is at ease. That is truly kusalā dhammā. What is akusalā dhammā? It is our mind not being good. When our mind is not good, then our conditioned body is not good, parents and siblings are not good, possessions, money, and gold are not good. This is akusalā dhammā. We are the ones who have done all of this, so we receive it. This is what we should examine, drawing it inwards to the Dhamma. We are the doers of all of it. If we had not done it, we would not have it. This is the practice.

title: Ajahn Funn: The Buddha, having fully awakened, explained why this is so. Having contemplated, he saw that beings do not achieve their aspirations because they…
content:
The Buddha Formulated the Dhamma on Our Very Selves

The Buddha, having fully awakened, explained why this is so. Having contemplated, he saw that beings do not achieve their aspirations because they lack action and lack practice. Therefore, he laid down this Dhamma. The Dhamma he laid down was not placed anywhere else; it was placed on our very selves. He formulated the Dhamma not elsewhere: the formulation of the aggregates (khandha-paññatti), the formulation of the sense spheres (āyatana-paññatti), the formulation of the elements (dhātu-paññatti), the formulation of the faculties (indriya-paññatti), the formulation of persons (puggala-paññatti). See, he formulated the Dhamma right here. He didn't formulate it anywhere else. The formulation of the aggregates – formulated from the aggregates: form, feeling, perception, mental formations, consciousness. This is what we dwell in, what we regard as a person. It's just the four elements. That's it: the five aggregates.

Then, the six sense spheres: eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, mind. The Dhamma he formulated relies on these: eye for seeing, ear for hearing, nose for smelling, tongue for tasting, body for touching, mind paired with mental objects. He formulated it like this. The arising of suffering and happiness – all arise from these.

title: Ajahn Funn: Now, are we a puññābhisaṅkhāra (wholesome formation) or apuññābhisaṅkhāra (unwholesome formation)? Is it kusala or akusala? What is kusala like? It is when…
content:
Samatha: Calming the Mind Like Still Water – Clear and Pure

Now, are we a puññābhisaṅkhāra (wholesome formation) or apuññābhisaṅkhāra (unwholesome formation)? Is it kusala or akusala? What is kusala like? It is when our mind drinks in happiness and ease, not concocting anything else. What is akusala like? Our mind is not good — it is miserable, troubled, agitated. Therefore, the Buddha laid down both samatha-kammaṭṭhāna (calm meditation) and vipassanā-kammaṭṭhāna (insight meditation) as instruction for us to know, to explain and show cause and effect so that we know good and evil. Samatha — what does it mean? Samatha is making the mind calm, calm from mental objects and perceptions (saññā).

When our mind is calm, it is like still water. Still water is clear. Our mind, when calm, is clear — clear and pure, free from suffering, free from faults, free from danger, free from enmity, free from evil and depravity, free from misery, poverty, deprivation, want, trouble, and annoyance — none of that. It is calm. When water is still, it sees itself, sees its own reflection, sees good and evil. When the mind becomes calm, it then gives rise to vipassanā-kammaṭṭhāna.

title: Ajahn Funn: And when we see these things in this way, how can we say that they’re our self? This takes us to the second…
content:
Whatever Is Inconstant Is Not-Self: The Second Round

And when we see these things in this way, how can we say that they’re our self?

This takes us to the second round: Whatever’s inconstant is not-self, it’s anattā. Rūpaṁ anattā, vedanā anattā, saññā anattā, saṅkhārā anattā, viññāṇaṁ anattā. They’re all not-self. We have to focus on contemplating them like this. Why view them as your self? They tend toward illness. Why do they tend toward illness? Why do they tend toward aging? Why do they tend toward death? They’re not-self. So focus on seeing them.

When you contemplate them, you’ll be able to let go of self-identity views, uncertainty about the body and the world, groping around, saying that this is your child, that’s your husband, that’s your wife, that’s your brother or sister—or groping around saying that virtue is over there, the Dhamma is over there. You can stop groping. You see things clearly and distinctly right inside you.

title: Ajahn Funn: When our mind has let go completely, abandoned completely, released completely, emptied completely, then danger and enmity are ended, evil and kamma are…
content:
When the Mind Has Let Go Completely, Danger and Enmity Are Ended

When our mind has let go completely, abandoned completely, released completely, emptied completely, then danger and enmity are ended, evil and kamma are ended. Because this one is no longer kamma — where would kamma come from? This one is no longer danger — where would danger come from? This one has no evil — where would evil come from? Focus and see. Bring your attention inward. Listen to how inwardly it concocts and fashions the saṃsāracakka — the wheel of wandering. Only when one becomes an Arahant is it said that one breaks the saṃsāracakka, which means breaking the perceptions and mental concoctions. This concocting and fashioning is the turning wheel. Idaṃ kho, bhikkhave, dukkhaṃ ariyasaccaṃ — jātipi dukkhā. That is the truth: birth, aging, sickness, and death are suffering. It is truly like this. When we have seen this, we then look at our own heart.

Now, from here on, let us all contemplate so that we know and see. Sanditthiko — the practitioner knows and sees for themselves. How could we not see? It is our own mind. Focus and look so that you know and see. Make it certain, make it direct and clear. Do not let others deceive you by saying "happiness is this way, suffering is that way, good is this, bad is that." There is no need to doubt. Is happiness real? Is suffering real? In our own mind, right as we sit here — know it! Is birth real? Is aging real? Know it! Is non-birth real? Is non-aging real? If no one creates realms and births, then there is no birth. When there is no birth, where would aging come from? So let us all strive to know and see from now on.

title: Ajahn Funn: We all know that making merit and wholesome deeds is not found in trees, mountains, or forests, nor in the sky or air.…
content:
Merit Is a Good Mind, a Happy Mind, a Comfortable Mind

We all know that making merit and wholesome deeds is not found in trees, mountains, or forests, nor in the sky or air. The Buddha taught: kāmāvacaraṃ kusalaṃ cittaṃ uppannaṃ hoti — it is this mind that is the wholesome and unwholesome state.

What is merit? It is this very self sitting here. The characteristic of merit is a good mind, a happy mind, a comfortable mind, a cool mind. So now, is your mind at ease? Each of you examine yourselves. While listening to the sermon, do not just listen to the sound. You must listen to the concrete and mental phenomena (rūpa-dhamma and nāma-dhamma) within yourselves.

Listening to rūpa-dhamma means listening to this physical body of ours. Why listen? Because we take this physical form to be a self, a being, a person, a "me", a "him", a woman, a man, a monk, a novice. Is it really so? Try to contemplate.

The teachings say: rūpaṃ aniccaṃ, vedanā aniccā, saññā aniccā, saṅkhārā aniccā, viññāṇaṃ aniccaṃ. Then how can anything be ours? All forms are impermanent. Feelings (vedanā) are impermanent. All mental formations (saṅkhārā) are impermanent. Consciousness (viññāṇa) is impermanent. When is it ever permanent?

title: Ajahn Funn: When we contemplate, separating out the elements, analyzing them, we then see there is no self. And so sakkāyadiṭṭhi is abandoned. The idea…
content:
Abandoning Self-View, Doubt, and Clinging to Rites – Letting Go of Form

When we contemplate, separating out the elements, analyzing them, we then see there is no self. And so sakkāyadiṭṭhi is abandoned. The idea of a being, a person is abandoned, let go. As for perceptions and mental objects, vicikicchā no longer exists. As for sīlabbata-parāmāsa (grasping at rites and rituals), the fondling that "this will be good, that will be good" – that no longer exists either. Thus it is abandoned.

When the mind has abandoned, it lets go of form. Letting go of form, it reaches the formless realm (arūpabhava). What is the formless realm like? The mind is completely empty, nothing at all. Only the knower remains.

This knowing is what is important. It is called "Buddha" – the one who knows.

Therefore, we come to seek knowledge. Since we want knowledge, we practice that knowing, contemplate that knowing. This knowing is not darkness, not brightness, not light, not delusion. When it is deluded, we know it. When it is dark, we know it. When it is bright, we know it. When it is happy, it knows. When it is suffering, it knows. Thus.

title: Ajahn Funn: As for virtue, all of us are already restrained. Body, speech, and mind are already normal, already calm and subdued. What remains is…
content:
Restraining the Mind: Established in Knowing, Not Sent Forward or Backward

As for virtue, all of us are already restrained. Body, speech, and mind are already normal, already calm and subdued. What remains is concentration. Concentration is the mind firmly established within. So try to establish it. What is a firmly established mind like? It does not send itself forward or backward, left or right, above or below. It is fixed right at knowing. Wherever knowing is, we focus and look right there. We don't need to search. Look at what is already present. It is present in the mind itself. However it is, look at it just like that. So that you may come to know your own state of being. If it is happy, you know it. If it is suffering, you know it. If it is neutral, you know it. If it is in samādhi – that is, empty of all perceptions and objects – you know that too. You know what level, what realm your mind has fallen into. For example, whether it has fallen into the sensual plane (kāmāvacara) or has transcended the sensual plane. Thus you know it.
